summary: Cadastral survey from the Musee du Quai Branly, Paris. 78 cords, 8 STRING (10%), 100% hits. Architecture 1-PAIRE, depth L2, 16 clusters, 5 colors (AB dominant at 28, W at 25). Vocabulary is overwhelmingly geographic: kaqa x3 (wall-AY — the MOST wall-focused khipu per text cord in the corpus), qaqa x2 (rock), piqa x1 (summit/who-TOPIC), paqa x1 (east), waqa x1 (huaca!). Five geographic terms in eight text cords = full cadastral vocabulary. OKR: C53 'tied loosely around main cord' = movable marker. Same museum as UR189 and UR192.
interpretation:
IDENTIFICATION
  78 cords, depth L2 — paired structure with subsidiaries.
  Architecture: 1-PAIRE, 16 clusters.
  Colors: AB(28), W(25), GG(11), MB(9), KB(5) — 5 colors.
  8 STRING (10%), 100% hit rate. 66 INT, 4 EMPTY.
  Provenance: Unknown.
  Museum: Musee du Quai Branly, Paris (same museum as UR189 and UR192).

COLOR-AS-ROLE
  AB (light brown, 28 cords = 36%): Primary survey entries — the land itself.
  W (white, 25 cords = 32%): Secondary entries — neutral/data.
  GG (grey-green, 11 cords = 14%): Justice/identity color — possibly marking boundaries with legal weight.
  MB (medium brown, 9 cords = 12%): Tertiary category.
  KB (khaki brown, 5 cords = 6%): Minor category.

KEY VOCABULARY
  kaqa x3 = wall (Aymara) — THREE walls named, each a boundary. The most wall-focused khipu per text cord in the corpus.
  qaqa x2 = rock — landmarks in the survey.
  piqa x1 = summit/head/who-TOPIC — a high point in the terrain.
  paqa x1 = east — directional orientation of the surveyed land.
  waqa x1 = huaca (sacred site) — the ritual anchor of the territory.

  Five geographic terms in eight text cords = 62.5% geographic vocabulary. This is a PURE CADASTRAL document.

OKR NOTES
  C53: 'tied loosely around main cord' = movable marker. Like a physical bookmark left by the surveyor — or the investigator — to mark a position in the register.

STRUCTURE
  78 cords in 16 clusters = ~4.9 cords per cluster. The L2 depth (paired subsidiaries) suggests a two-level hierarchy: boundary section > plot entries.
  66 INT cords (85%) carry the numerical measurements — areas, distances, tribute values.
  4 EMPTY cords = section separators.
  8 STRING cords (10%) carry the vocabulary that maps the terrain.

CROSS-CORPUS — QUAI BRANLY TRIO
  UR188, UR189, and UR192 are all at the Musee du Quai Branly. Their relationship is unknown but museum co-location often indicates shared provenance or acquisition history.

NARRATIVE
  Three walls. That is what defines this survey — the Aymara word kaqa, three times, more than any other khipu in the corpus per text cord. Between the walls: rocks (qaqa, twice), a summit (piqa), the eastern direction (paqa), and a huaca (waqa) — the sacred place that anchors the survey to the ritual landscape.

  Cord 53 was tied loosely around the main cord, a movable marker left by the surveyor — or the investigator — like a bookmark in a register of terrain. At the Musee du Quai Branly, alongside UR189 and UR192, this cadastral survey maps a territory defined by its walls.

  Seventy-eight cords, sixteen clusters, five colors. The grey-green cords (GG, eleven of them) carry the justice/identity function — these may mark the boundaries that have legal force, the walls that are not merely physical but juridical, separating one community's land from another. The light brown and white cords carry the measurements: sixty-six numerical entries quantifying the terrain that the eight text cords name. A summit to the east, rocks as landmarks, a huaca as the spiritual center, and three walls enclosing the whole — this is a land survey in the fullest Andean sense, where geography and the sacred are the same thing.
references: Sivan 2026, ALBA Project — Cadastral survey analysis, geographic vocabulary concentration
Musee du Quai Branly, Paris — UR188 alongside UR189 and UR192
Cross-corpus: UR189, UR192 (Quai Branly khipus — co-located, possible shared provenance)
OKR: C53 'tied loosely around main cord' — movable marker notation
